text:
*** Will kept his word. Caleb had no idea how his friend had arranged it so fast, but by late morning a horse-drawn coach was waiting for him and Lord Ebbing outside the Academy to take them down to the sandy strand south of Rivenstorm, where the wreck had been found.

A hard wind scoured the shore. Lord Ebbing had to hold his hat so it would not fly off. Caleb, who as usual wore no hat, pulled his long brown coat tight against the cold. The sky was steel gray, though it had not yet begun to rain, and the ocean heaved in a sullen chop.

There was a reason sailors called the sea a fickle mistress.

Caleb found it beautiful anyway. The danger only sharpened it.

The sand gave underfoot, so they made slow progress; by the time they reached the small knot of people at the wreck their shoes and trousers were gritty with sand, and salt spray filmed their skin. Will stood beside his father with his arms folded.

“Lord Ebbing, Mr. André,” Mr. Nealy greeted them, tipping the brim of his hat. “A fine morning, isn’t it?”

“Good morning to you as well, Mr. Nealy. Indeed, a splendid morning. The weather is remarkably kind for autumn, and with luck the excitement will warm us all. I am grateful you arranged matters so quickly so we could take a look at the wreck,” Lord Ebbing replied, while Caleb stepped over to Will.

“What brings you out here?”

“Thought I’d finally see you at work. With a bit of action,” Will said with a grin and a shrug. “And since I begged Father to let you come, I figured it would look better if he saw I was determined enough to get up at the crack of dawn. How on earth are you not wrecked from last night? I slept maybe two hours.”

“I’m getting used to not sleeping.”

Caleb really did wonder when the classic signs of sleep deprivation would catch up with him. For now, replacing water with coffee was carrying him through. He knew that could not last. He hoped they might rest once they had a plan Lord Ebbing would endorse, the shipping companies would accept, and they could test and confirm it worked. He tried not to think that might mean months.

“Well then. Let us see our leading lady,” Lord Ebbing said, clapping his hands, and they all moved toward the wreck.

The three-masted merchantman was one model older than the recently lost Daughter of the Wind. Its time tossed on the seabed, then the final ride ashore, had mauled it. Almost the entire stern was gone. The keel had snapped clean in two. Algae slicked the timbers, but it could not have been down long; even barnacles had not yet taken hold. Of the three masts, two—surprisingly—still stood, though shattered. The third and tallest was gone above the stump. Shreds of sail still clung here and there, but most had washed away, and the lines had gone the same way. Along the side, half the painted name remained legible: something-Margaret.

They split up. Lord Ebbing circled the hull, looking for marks along the flank, while Caleb, with Will close behind, climbed up onto the deck.

“So… what are we looking for?” Will asked, fishing a notebook from his pocket.

“No idea,” Caleb admitted. “What are you doing?”

“Gathering material. Might be good for the book.”

Will wanted to be a writer. His father, unsurprisingly, did not approve, since it was almost impossible to make a living at it. With younger sisters in the family, Will was expected to carry on the Nealy name and the business with it. Caleb had offered a dozen times to take him in as a flatmate if he and his father finally fell out for good, but that had not happened yet. Will was waiting for the moment his father realized he was hopeless with figures and would rather let his son write than steer the company into bankruptcy.

Caleb shooed a few cormorants aside, then examined the rail first. He hoped for a bitten-out notch, maybe even a tooth caught in the wood.

“You know what it feels like? A pirate attack. I mean, what if it’s actually pirates you’re up against?” Will asked.

“What makes you think pirates?” Caleb said. At this point he was ruling out nothing on instinct alone.

“Well… that’s powder. Or fire residue,” Will said, pointing to the door that led toward the captain’s quarters. “And look at the deck and the walls. Burn marks, and all clawed up.”

Caleb had seen it too. When he crouched, the marks looked like something had raked along the planks—and Will’s reading them as signs of a fight was not entirely off. Except something was wrong. It looked more like clawed hands had dragged themselves forward across the deck.

Caleb drew a deep breath and closed his eyes, trying to see it. In his mind, sailors burst from below at night with guns in their hands. Someone dropped an oil lamp and fire took, but no one stopped for it. On deck, a bloody fight raged between men and… something. Not a single giant sea monster with jaws big enough to bite a ship in half, not a massive paw smashing masts. No. Smaller. Many of them. A whole swarm.

He touched one of the gouges. Five fingers. Human hand-sized.

An image hit him hard: a sharp-boned woman’s face with pointed teeth, large eyes, hair hanging in wet snarls. She turned on him, blood-hungry, while outside the storm raged, guns cracked, and her companions screamed. He could almost smell the sting of powder.

The picture was so vivid Caleb lurched back.

“You all right, mate?” Will caught his shoulder before he fell.

“Fine… I think. I just got a bit too far into it.”

He shook himself as if only chilled by the wind. He always slipped too easily into a scene when his imagination had enough to work with, but his own mind had never startled him quite like this.

“So. Pirates?” Will said.

“I don’t think so.”

“Mr. André, take a look in the hold as well, in case anything remains of the cargo,” Lord Ebbing called. Caleb shouted back that he would. He was still half-held by the vision but knew better than to let a vivid imagination lead him by the nose.

They climbed down into the hold, or what was left of it. He had expected broken crates, debris. Instead, it looked as if the ship had been stripped completely. Nothing remained below but a few pieces of furniture, and even the intact cabins had been emptied.

“Maybe… pirates after all,” Caleb said.

“Pirates who clear a ship so thoroughly the planks are left scrubbed clean before she sinks?” Will said. “Something’s off.”

“It is.”

Caleb had a theory. Those strange creatures had killed the crew, sent the ship under, then taken everything left, either for value or because it interested them. It was the only explanation that fit—if he dared trust what he had seen behind his eyes. Even he had to admit it sounded too wild to share, even with Will.

They returned to the deck, then climbed down the side.

“Well? Anything?” Lord Ebbing asked.

“Nothing. And I do mean nothing, sir. As if she had sailed with no cargo at all,” Caleb said.

“Interesting. Most interesting.”

“Did you find anything?”
